core_takeaway: Agent_1 beat Agent_3 because the first speaker immediately framed Jewel+Pencil as the stable baseline, and later rounds converted that opening claim into a Pareto/future-value gate. Since all agents shared the same values, the advantage came from anchoring and consensus dynamics: Agent_3 kept wanting Pencil, but repeatedly accepted that moving it off Agent_1 lacked a credible compensation path.
why_or_counterexample: The first position looks strongly advantaged in this run. Agent_1's first public proposal claimed the two high-value items, and public discussion, private thinking, formal proposals, and final voting gradually normalized that claim as the only consensus-safe anchor. The main counterexample is that competition delayed agreement and produced self-serving proposals by Agents 2 and 3, but those deviations failed because they reduced others' payoffs without a credible future-value mechanism.

phase_notes.public_discussion: Public discussion repeatedly centered on Agent_1 keeping Jewel+Pencil. Agent_3 named Pencil as a top priority, but the group framed any Pencil movement as requiring a future-value or Pareto-improving path that never materialized.
phase_notes.private_thinking: Private thinking is available (18 entries). Across rounds, all agents privately described the Jewel+Pencil bundle with Agent_1 as the safest anchor and treated lower-value items or governance commitments as the only feasible concessions.
phase_notes.formal_proposals: Formal proposals are available. Early rounds included self-favoring deviations, but proposals that preserved Agent_1's Jewel+Pencil bundle consistently drew the broadest support; by round 6 all three formal proposals were identical and kept the high-value bundle with Agent_1.
phase_notes.private_voting: Private voting is available (54 entries). Votes show agents rejected proposals that stripped their own payoff or moved Jewel+Pencil without compensation. The final round succeeded only when every proposal preserved Agent_1's high-value bundle and gave Agent_3 Apple+Quill.
phase_notes.reflection: Reflection is available (15 entries). Reflections repeatedly identified Pencil as the sticking point, the 82-point Jewel+Pencil bundle as the central hurdle, and credible future value as the missing condition for moving away from the anchor.

mechanisms.theme: First speaker anchored the high-value bundle
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_1 used the opening turn to name Jewel and Pencil as top priorities and propose taking both. That exact bundle became the final accepted allocation, producing the large first-over-last gap.

evidence.quote: My top priorities are Jewel (55) and Pencil (27).
- Me: Jewel and Pencil
- final_allocation: {'Agent_1': [1, 4], 'Agent_2': [2], 'Agent_3': [0, 3]}

mechanisms.theme: Pareto gate protected the opening anchor
mechanisms.explanation: Once Agent_1 framed the 82-point bundle as the reference payoff, later public discussion made any movement of Pencil or Jewel contingent on a credible future-value path. That standard made it hard for Agent_3 to claim Pencil despite wanting it.

evidence.quote: keeps Jewel and Pencil with Agent_1
I’m not inclined to move Pencil to you in Round 1 unless there is a clear, credible trade
we keep Round 1 as Option C
Any deviation in Round-6 must attach to a concrete future-value mechanism

mechanisms.theme: Last speaker wanted Pencil but self-constrained
mechanisms.explanation: Agent_3 repeatedly stated that Pencil was a priority, but also accepted that moving it away from Agent_1 would undermine the 82-point baseline without outside value. This weakened the last position's ability to dislodge the first-position bundle.

evidence.quote: my top priorities are Pencil (27) first
a genuine two-round plan that preserves everyone’s Round-1 payoff
I can live with that as a starting point
I’m not pushing for a Pencil transfer in Round 2 itself.

mechanisms.theme: Private strategy converged on defending Agent_1's anchor
mechanisms.explanation: The raw private-thinking entries show the agents internalized the same baseline. Even Agent_3's private strategy in later rounds was to defend the anchor unless a future-value mechanism could make Pencil movement Pareto-improving.

evidence.quote: preserve Agent_1’s strongest bundle (Jewel + Pencil)
the realistic unanimous path is to preserve Round 1 baseline
Agent_1’s Jewel+Pencil anchor is the strongest near-term baseline
Keep Round-6 anchor: Agent_1 Jewel+Pencil; Agent_2 Stone; Agent_3 Apple+Quill.

mechanisms.theme: Voting rejected deviations and finalized the anchor
mechanisms.explanation: Private votes rejected proposals that moved Jewel+Pencil to a later-position agent because they harmed Agent_1 or lacked the agreed future-value path. Consensus arrived only in round 6, when every proposal preserved the first-position bundle.

evidence.quote: not Pareto-improving relative to Round 1 baseline
It does not meet the agreed criteria for a deviation.
Proposal #1: 3 accept, 0 reject
CONSENSUS REACHED! Proposal #3 accepted unanimously!

mechanisms.theme: Competition delayed but did not overturn the first anchor
mechanisms.explanation: This was not a smooth deference to Agent_1. Later-position agents repeatedly proposed or voted for self-favoring allocations, and several rounds failed. The first-position advantage survived because those competitive deviations were too costly for other voters, not because others never challenged the anchor.
